intro: TelemetryDeck is freemium with a free tier; OpenIAP is free and open source. TelemetryDeck: Privacy-focused mobile app analytics with GDPR-compliant signal tracking and RevenueCat integration for subscription metrics. OpenIAP: Open cross-platform IAP specification unifying types, error codes and purchase flows across iOS StoreKit and Google Play Billing. TelemetryDeck fits indie Apple-platform developers wanting privacy-friendly product analytics. OpenIAP fits OSS library authors and engineers standardizing IAP across platforms. Both land at 4/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency on our rubric. The honest trade-off: TelemetryDeck's main drawback — Not a full product analytics platform; OpenIAP's — Spec still maturing, breaking changes possible. One point in TelemetryDeck's favour: Privacy-first, GDPR-friendly signal tracking. For OpenIAP: Open spec unifying IAP types across platforms. TelemetryDeck is explicitly not the right pick for cross-platform apps needing deep funnels and behavioral analytics. OpenIAP is not aimed at product teams looking for a ready-made subscription service. Another TelemetryDeck advantage: Affordable vs Mixpanel and Amplitude. Another OpenIAP advantage: Cuts per-platform conditional code for teams.
quick_take: TelemetryDeck is for indie Apple-platform developers wanting privacy-friendly product analytics; OpenIAP is for OSS library authors; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose TelemetryDeck if indie Apple-platform developers wanting privacy-friendly product analytics, and if portability and keeping the exit door open matter.
verdict_b: Choose OpenIAP if OSS library authors and engineers standardizing IAP across platforms, and if you want to avoid recurring license costs and are willing to self-host.

faq.a: No: OpenIAP is free and open source, while TelemetryDeck runs on freemium pricing. TelemetryDeck may still be cheaper in total cost once you factor in hosting, maintenance, and engineering time to run OpenIAP yourself.

faq.q: Can I migrate from TelemetryDeck to OpenIAP?
faq.a: Migrating out of TelemetryDeck is straightforward — it's rated low lock-in (Privacy-first, signals exportable). The harder question is how deeply you commit to OpenIAP afterward, since it sits at high lock-in (open spec for IAP). Plan the forward cost, not just the exit.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, TelemetryDeck or OpenIAP?
faq.a: Both score 4/5 for developer experience in our rubric, so neither has a structural edge. The practical answer depends on stack fit: TelemetryDeck's ergonomics suit some workflows, OpenIAP's suit others. Try both on a throwaway project before committing.

faq.q: Is OpenIAP a good alternative to TelemetryDeck?
faq.a: They sit in the same category, so yes — OpenIAP is a plausible alternative for many TelemetryDeck use cases. It fits best when OSS library authors and engineers standardizing IAP across platforms. Skip it if product teams looking for a ready-made subscription service.
